title: Publish Equivalent Media Information
summary: Build and verify accessible media equivalents in multilingual heat-advisory media release through prediction, inspection, explanation, cumulative code, and changed-case transfer.
objectives: Explain accessible media equivalents from purpose, behavior, and evidence.
Apply accessible media equivalents without discarding earlier valid work.
Diagnose the named misconception in changed content, input, and failure states.

options.text: multilingual heat-advisory media release phase 1.1: inventory speech, meaningful sound, visual-only action, text, and interactive controls
multilingual heat-advisory media release phase 1.2: author captions, transcript, and audio-description support for the relevant information
multilingual heat-advisory media release phase 1.3: review timing, speakers, terminology, reading order, and control access
multilingual heat-advisory media release phase 1.4: complete the same information task with sound off, visuals unavailable, and keyboard only

steps.title: 04 · Read the multilingual heat-advisory media release case · theory
steps.instruction: Checkpoint 4: Which claim preserves accessible media equivalents when data, input, and layout change? Scenario: multilingual heat-advisory media release.
steps.why: Publish Equivalent Media Information makes the decision observable and testable. This is checkpoint 4 in multilingual heat-advisory media release.

content.text: Captions communicate dialogue and meaningful sound; transcripts provide an equivalent text path; audio description conveys important visual information; and text or image alternatives must preserve the task-relevant meaning. Automatic output requires human review for timing, speakers, terminology, and critical sounds. Controls must remain operable and media must not autoplay unexpectedly. Apply it to multilingual heat-advisory media release.

Multilingual heat-advisory media release case 4: provide a filename as the transcript because users can infer the spoken content.
